title: St. John the Evangelist (Departure) — St. Arsenius the Great & St. Emilia
narrative:
The Blessed Repose of St. John the Beloved Apostle and Evangelist, St. Arsenius the Great Ascetic of Egypt, and St. Emilia of Caesarea the Mother of Saints

On the eighth of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the repose — the blessed departure — of the holy, glorious, and all-praised Apostle and Evangelist John the Theologian, the Beloved Disciple, the last surviving member of the Twelve, who alone among the apostles died not by the sword of the persecutor but by the gentle dissolving of extreme old age in the city of Ephesus. The tradition of John's departure is theologically significant precisely because of its unlikeness to every other apostolic death: where Peter was crucified, where Paul was beheaded, where Andrew was crucified, where Thomas was pierced by spears, John simply lay down in the earth he had asked his disciples to dig for him and breathed his last with a prayer of thanksgiving on his lips. This death without violence is not a lesser death but a greater mystery: the tradition sees in it the fulfilment of Christ's mysterious word to Peter about the Beloved Disciple — If it is my will that he remain until I come, what is that to you? — a word that was not a promise of physical immortality but a promise of a different kind of fidelity, the fidelity of the one who remains, who witnesses, who outlasts every storm.

The theology of John's repose in the Ethiopian tradition is closely connected to the theology of his Gospel: the evangelist of the Incarnation, who begins with the Word existing before all creation and ends with the world itself being insufficient to contain the books that could be written about what the Word did, departs from this world in a manner consistent with his theological vision. John did not fight death; he entered it as he had entered the empty tomb — leaning forward, looking intently, receiving what he found there as gift. His repose is the tradition's icon of the holy death, the death that is not a defeat but a consummation, the death in which the one who has truly received the Gospel discovers that the Gospel was always about this moment: the moment of laying down the body with thanksgiving and passing through into the fullness of the Resurrection that one has spent a lifetime proclaiming.

Saint Arsenius the Great is the giant of Egyptian monasticism who stands alongside the reposing John as a second image of the contemplative life taken to its uttermost limit. A Roman of noble birth, a tutor to the sons of the Emperor Theodosius, Arsenius heard a divine voice telling him to flee from men and to be silent, and obeyed with a literalness that transformed him from one of the most socially prominent figures in the empire into the most hidden of the desert fathers. He was famous for the extreme brevity of his speech — the sayings attributed to him are among the most terse in the entire collection of the Apophthegmata — and for a particular habit the tradition records with affectionate detail: he wept so continuously during prayer that his eyelashes had fallen out from the constant tears. His maxim — I have often repented of having spoken, but never of having been silent — is the Johannine theology of the Word expressed in its negative form: the Word is so precious that all other words are better not spoken.

Saint Emilia of Caesarea completes this day's assembly with a figure whose holiness was expressed not in solitary contemplation but in the extraordinary fruitfulness of her motherhood. Emilia was the mother of Basil the Great, Gregory of Nyssa, Peter of Sebaste, and Macrina the Elder — four children who became saints, three of them among the most significant theologians in the history of Christianity. The tradition honours her not merely as the biological cause of this remarkable generation but as the formative theological presence in their lives: it was Emilia and her mother Macrina the Elder who first taught the children of Caesarea to pray, to read Scripture, to fast, and to understand the Christian life as a sustained orientation toward God. The woman who departed this life surrounded by her saint-children is the tradition's answer to the solitary repose of John and the solitary weeping of Arsenius: the holy life takes many forms, and the mother who forms saints is no less a contemplative than the evangelist who writes eternity into prose. Amen.
